title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear a conservationist called Emma Collins discussing her experiences with wildlife research in the Amazon rainforest. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear a conservationist called Emma Collins discussing her experiences with wildlife research in the Amazon rainforest. For questions 7 – 14, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: Emma Collins talks about her experiences with wildlife research in the Amazon rainforest.
extracts.audio_script:
Hello everyone, my name is Emma Collins and today I'd like to share with you my remarkable journey in the field of wildlife conservation, particularly my research experience in the Amazon rainforest. My passion for wildlife first ignited during a family holiday when I was [7]eight years old[/7]. We visited a local nature reserve that had such a diverse range of animal species, and I was immediately fascinated. That single experience planted the seed for what would grow into a lifelong commitment to conservation. 

So, fast forward a bit to my university years, where I chose to study biology with a special focus on ecology and conservation. It was during this time that I got the opportunity to join a research project in the Amazon rainforest. My first impression, as you might imagine, was one of awe. The [8]breathtaking biodiversity[/8] in the Amazon simply left me speechless. Working there, I got to observe the interplay of numerous species in their natural habitats—a spectacle that words often fail to describe.

One of the primary goals of our research was to study the impact of climate variations on local wildlife. I was particularly involved in monitoring amphibian populations. These creatures, as we've learned, are really good indicators of environmental health. If you ever heard as a child about the proverbial [9]canary in a coal mine[/9], the amphibians have a similar role in their ecosystems. 

Throughout my time there, I faced numerous challenges. One of the most pressing issues was the harsh weather conditions. I recall one incident when a sudden thunderstorm led to [10]flooded research stations[/10], which quite literally washed away weeks of gathered data. It was a real lesson in resilience and adaptability. 

Another significant challenge was the occasional difficulty in communication. Being in such a remote area made it hard to maintain [11]reliable internet access[/11], which was crucial for maintaining updated logs and studies. It taught us the importance of preparation and digital communication planning before embarking into the field. 

Our findings over the two years indicated some distressing trends. We noted a decline in certain amphibian species, which was directly linked to [12]habitat loss[/12] due to deforestation. Addressing these issues on a broader scale requires cooperation with local and international organizations.

Having said all that, despite the challenges and sometimes alarming discoveries, the overall experience was invaluable. It solidified my resolve to make a difference. I'm currently working on a project focused on [13]restoration efforts[/13] for degraded environments, specifically aiming to recreate lost habitats that various species depend on.

Before I conclude, let me say that for anyone looking to follow a similar path, that a deep passion for the subject and perseverance will be your most invaluable tools. Surround yourself with like-minded individuals because collaborative effort is key in conservation. I also cannot overstate the importance of field experience—it’s something that gives you insights beyond textbooks.

To wrap up, although my journey has been challenging, it has also been incredibly fulfilling. So, if you're someone who cares deeply about our natural world, I strongly urge you to consider getting involved, whether through [14]volunteer programmes[/14] or even local conservation efforts. Thank you for your attention and I'm excited to hear any thoughts or questions you might have.

dialogue.speaker: emma
